title: Map-Reduce Prompting
concept: Applying the same prompt across many chunks in parallel (map) then combining results (reduce).

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: in a Sequential Prompt Chain, what happens to the output of Step 1?

options.text: It gets thrown away and Step 2 starts fresh
It becomes the input for Step 2
It runs at the same time as Step 2

exercises.explain: In a sequential chain, each step feeds its output into the next step, like a relay race baton — Step 1's answer becomes Step 2's starting material.

cards.title: 500 Pages, One Question
cards.body: Imagine a 500-page report and you need a summary — but it's way too big to paste into one prompt. What if you could split it into chunks, summarize each piece at the same time, then combine those mini-summaries into one final answer?

cards.title: What Is Map-Reduce Prompting?
cards.body: Map-Reduce means applying the SAME prompt to many chunks of data separately (the 'map' step), then combining all those results into one final output (the 'reduce' step). Unlike sequential chains, the map step can happen all at once, in parallel.

cards.title: The Fruit-Sorting Crew
cards.body: Imagine 10 friends each sorting one basket of mixed fruit into piles (that's the map step — same task, done separately on different baskets). Then one friend combines all the piles into one giant sorted table (that's the reduce step).

cards.title: Summarizing a Long Book
cards.body: Map step: split the book into 20 chapters, and ask the same prompt 'Summarize this chapter in 3 sentences' on each chapter separately. Reduce step: feed all 20 mini-summaries into a final prompt that says 'Combine these into one overall summary.'

cards.title: Myth: Map-Reduce Is Just a Chain
cards.body: Not quite! A sequential chain is step-by-step, like a single line of dominoes. Map-Reduce splits the work into many identical parallel tasks first, THEN merges them — more like many dominoes falling at once, then someone sweeping them into one pile.

cards.title: Sequential Chain vs. Map-Reduce
cards.body: Both break big tasks into smaller prompts, but they organize the work very differently.

left.text: Different prompts, one after another: extract → summarize → format. Order matters, each step depends on the last.

right.text: Same prompt run on many chunks at once (map), then a final prompt merges all results (reduce). Chunks don't depend on each other.

exercises.prompt: You need to find the total sentiment (positive/negative) across 1,000 customer reviews. Which is the map step?

options.text: Running the same 'classify sentiment' prompt on each review separately
Combining all 1,000 sentiment labels into one final report
Reading the final report to a manager
Deleting reviews that are too short

exercises.explain: The map step applies the same prompt (classify sentiment) to each individual chunk (each review) separately. Combining the results afterward is the reduce step, not the map step.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In Map-Reduce prompting, the chunks processed in the map step must be handled in a strict, dependent order.
exercises.explain: False — a key benefit of map-reduce is that each chunk is processed independently, so they can run in any order or even at the same time (in parallel). Only the reduce step needs all the map results gathered together.

exercises.sentence: After the map step summarizes each chunk separately, the reduce step must combine all those mini-summaries into one final summary.
exercises.explain: The reduce step's job is to combine the many separate map results into a single unified output.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three plans is the AI-generated Map-Reduce plan for summarizing a 300-page document?

options.text: Split the document into 15 chunks, run 'summarize this chunk in 3 sentences' on each chunk in parallel, then run 'combine these 15 summaries into one summary' on the results
Read the document once, then ask a follow-up question about page 47
Translate the document into French, then ask someone to proofread it

exercises.explain: Option A is the map-reduce pattern: the same prompt applied identically across many independent chunks (map), followed by a merging prompt (reduce). The others are single-pass tasks or unrelated workflows, not map-reduce.

exercises.prompt: A company has 50 separate customer support transcripts and wants one master list of the top 5 recurring complaints. They also remember from the last lesson that sequential chains pipe output step-by-step. Which approach best fits Map-Reduce (not sequential chaining)?

options.text: Feed all 50 transcripts into one giant prompt at once and hope it fits
Extract complaints from transcript 1, then feed that output into a prompt for transcript 2, and so on for all 50
Run the same 'extract key complaints' prompt on each of the 50 transcripts independently, then run a final prompt that merges all 50 complaint lists into the top 5
Manually read all 50 transcripts without using prompts

exercises.explain: This is Map-Reduce: the map step applies the identical 'extract key complaints' prompt to each transcript independently (they don't need each other's output), and the reduce step combines all 50 results into a final merged answer. Option B describes a sequential chain instead, where each step depends on the previous one — much slower and unnecessary here since the transcripts are independent. Option A would likely overflow context limits, and option D skips prompting entirely. Map-Reduce shines exactly in cases like this: many independent chunks needing the same treatment, then a smart merge.
